Facts.text: Leo VIII elected Pope
Béla I is crowned King of Hungary in Székesfehérvár
Jean Bodels' mystic play "Jeu de St Nicholas" premieres in Arras
Northern Dutch coast flooded, the "Saint-Nicolas Flood"
Quito, Ecuador, founded by Spanish colonists on the ruins of an Inca city
Don Francisco de Mello appointed land guardian of South Netherlands
Pride's Purge: Thomas Pride prevents 96 presbyterians from sitting in English parliament
1st play in American colonies acted by professional players in New York
First recorded appendectomy performed by Claudius Amyand at St George's Hospital in London
Laurens Pieter van de Speigel is appointed Dutch pension advisor
The U.S. Congress moves from New York City to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Amsterdam-Utrecht railway opens
Alpha Sigma Phi Fraternity is founded at Yale College.
Battle of Deveaux's Neck, South Carolina
13th Amendment of the United States Constitution is ratified, abolishing slavery except as a punishment for crime where the party has been duly convicted
Chicago water supply tunnel 3,227 m into Lake Michigan completed
Colored National Labor Union, 1st Black labor convention meets at Union League Hall in Washington, D.C.
1st international football game in US: Yale 2, Eton (England) 1 in New Haven, Connecticut
1st crematorium in US begins operation, Washington, Pennsylvania
Washington Post publishes 1st edition
Atmosphere of Venus detected during transit
Aluminum capstone is set atop the Washington Monument in Washington, D.C., making it the tallest man-made structure in the world, overtaking Cologne Cathedral
Japanese Zen Buddist D. T. Suzuki has an awakening at Engakuji temple, in Kamakura
London becomes the world's first city to host licenced taxicabs
Sumatra Atjehs guerilla leader Panglima Polim surrenders
British grant Transvaal self-government
Coal mine explosions in Monongah, West Virginia kills 361
China votes for universal human rights
White Sox beat Giants 9-4 in exhibition game in Tokyo
German troops over run Lodz
World War I: The Central Powers under General Mackensen capture Bucharest

French munition ship SS Mont Blanc collides with Norwegian SS Imo in Halifax harbour, Canada, resulting explosion kills at least 1,700 and injures more than 9,000 people - world’s largest pre-atomic explosion
Taking advantage of the temporary relaxation of authority in Russia, Finland declares itself a republic, following the Ukraine on 20 November
1st constitution of Irish Free State comes into operation
1st electric power line commercial carrier in US, Utica, NY
Italy, Britain and Egypt sign Jaghbub accord, ceding the previous Libyan territory of Jaghbub to Egypt
Record 73,000 pay to watch Chicago Bears beat NY Giants 19-7
The Banana Massacre: Colombian troops fire on striking United Fruit Company workers in Ciénaga, killing about 1,000 people
Turkey introduces female suffrage
117 Spanish knights under capt Piet Laros return to Netherlands
French-German friendship treaty drawn up (Ribbentrop-Bonnet Pact)
5th Heisman Trophy Award: Nile Kinnick, Iowa (HB)
Gestapo arrest German resistance fighter/poster artist Helen Ernst
Dutch & British pilots see Japanese invasion fleet at Singapore
New York City Council agrees to build Idlewild Airport (now JFK) in Queens
RAF bombs Philips factory (150 die)
US 95th Infantry division reaches Westwall
Czech government tells Israeli ambassador that he is persona non grata
Brown's Lou "Toe" Groza kicks 8 PATs, beating Giants 62-14
New York psychologist Joyce Brothers wins "$64,000 Question" with topic of boxing

Against the background of the Soviet invasion of Hungary the nations square off at the Melbourne Olympics in a famous water polo match; game called off with Hungary leading 4-0 and near riot halted by police; Hungary goes on to win gold medal
Australian swimmer David Theile sets world record 1:02.2 to win the 100m backstroke gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics
German WWII prisoner of war Helmut Bantz and Soviet gymnast Valentin Muratov tie for the vault gold medal with 18.85 points at the Melbourne Olympics
Randhir Singh Gentle scores the winner as India beats Pakistan, 1-0 in the Melbourne Olympics field hockey final to win it's 6th consecutive gold medal in the sport
Soviet gymnast Viktor Chukarin follows his parallel bars gold medal with the individual all-round title and teams gold at the Melbourne Olympics
AFL-CIO votes to expel Teamsters (readmitted in October 1987)
First US attempt to launch a satellite fails when the Vanguard rocket explodes
Indonesia begins nationalizing Dutch possessions
US lunar probe Pioneer 3 reaches 107,269 km, falls back
MLB American League grants American entertainer and businessman Gene Autry a franchise: Los Angeles Angels
US abandons Skybolt ballistic missile program
Test Cricket debut of Graeme Pollock at the Gabba
The Beatles begin a tradition of releasing a Christmas record for fan club members
"Rudolph The Red-Nosed Reindeer" 1st airs on TV
KTVR TV channel 13 in La Grande, OR (PBS) begins broadcasting
2 trucks crash into a crowd of dancers in Sotouboua Togo, kills 125
Pakistan's Islamic Ideology Advisory Committee recommends that Islamic Studies be made a compulsory subject for Muslim students from primary to graduate level.
USSR performs nuclear test at Sary Shagan, USSR
Major League Baseball dismisses Commissioner William Eckert after 3 years
PBA National Bowling Championship won by Wayne Zahn (29)
WKID (WSCV) TV channel 51 in Fort Lauderdale, Florida (IND) 1st broadcast
USSR performs nuclear test
Cleveland Cavaliers 1st NBA home victory, beating Buffalo Braves 108-106
A woman dies trying to salvage property from the Salvation Army Citadel in Belfast after bomb which started a large fire in an adjoining building
Lewis Franklin Powell confirmed as US Supreme Court justice
Bahrain's constitution goes into effect
NL votes to move San Diego Padres to Washington, D.C. (doesn't happen)
41st Heisman Trophy Award: Archie Griffin, Ohio State (RB)
Balcombe Street Siege: for 6 days, four Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteers hold two hostages at an apartment in London, England
South Africa grants Bophuthatswana independence
Spain adopts a constitution
NASA launches Intelsat V satellite, no. 502
Rob de Castella of Australia sets Marathon record at 2:08:18
Droppin Well bombing: 11 British soldiers and 6 civilians are killed by an Irish National Liberation Army (INLA) time bomb at the Droppin’ Well Bar in Ballykelly, County Londonderry
A bomb planted on a bus in Jerusalem explodes, kills 6 Israelis
France performs a nuclear test
Hijackers aboard Kuwaiti jetliner kill 2nd hostage
52nd Heisman Trophy Award: Vinny Testaverde, Miami Fla (QB)
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
3 satanist Missouri teenagers bludgeon comrade to death for "fun"
Christa Rothenburger skates female world record 500m (39.39 sec)
Agnes Neil Williams purchases Baltimore Orioles for $70 million Eli Jacobs becomes CEO of Baltimore Orioles
Carlos Andres Perez re-elected president of Venezuela
Merv Hughes takes 13 wickets v WI at the WACA but Australia lose
Milwaukee Bucks win their 1,000th NBA game (2nd fastest)
Nelson Mandelais transferred to Victor Vester Prison, Capetown
STS-27 Atlantis lands in California after secret mission
Mafia drug kingpin bombs security force at Bogota, kills 52
Worst Canadian mass murder: Marc Lepine kills 14 women at U Montreal
Baseball player "Shoeless" Joe Jackson's signature sells, at auction, for $23,100
NHL grants conditional membership to Tampa Bay Lightning
"Star Trek VI-Undiscovered Country" premieres
300,000 hindus destroy mosque of Babri India, 4 die
Gunda Niemann skates ladies world record 5 km 7:13.29
Maltese Falcon auctioned for $398,590
Orange County California files for bankruptcy
Warner Brothers announces a 5th TV network in US, to begin on Jan 11, 1995
Mashonaland defeat England in first-class tour match
The Canadian province of Newfoundland is renamed Newfoundland and Labrador
Several villagers are shot dead during protests in Dongzhou, China
NASA reveals photographs taken by Mars Global Surveyor that suggest the presence of liquid water on Mars
7 people are killed and 770 injured during Egyptian protests
A 243 million year old Nyasasaurus fossil is discovered in Tanzania
Typhoon Bopha’s death toll rise to 418 with 318 missing and 179,000 displaced
A 2-year old boy dies from Ebola in Meliandou, Guinea
"Supermassive" most distant black hole discovery announced by astronomers in journal "Nature", 13 billion light-years away, 800 x bigger than the Sun
Brazilian drug lord Rogério da Silva arrested in Rio
Britain's top Brexit negotiator David Davis admits there has been "no systematic impact assessment" on effects of Brexit
Skirball wildfire near Los Angeles closes the Interstate 405 as thousands forced to evacuate
Starbucks opens its largest cafe in the world in Shanghai, 30,000 square-feet, 1/2 size of a football field
Time Magazine names their Person of the Year "The Silence Breakers", people who came forward to report sexual misconduct
Luxembourg is the first country to make all public transport free
Oldest-known plague sample is found in 4,900-year-old remains of a 20-year-old woman in Gökhem, southern Sweden, published in "Cell"
Senegal's new Museum of Black Civilisations is opened in Dakar by President Macky Sall
Japanese space capsule Hayabusa-2 lands safely back on earth in Australia, carrying first significant rock samples from an asteroid, the Ryugu
US PresidentDonald Trumporders about 700 troops withdrawn from Somalia
New York Mayor Bill de Blasio announces a COVID-19 mandate for all private employers effective December 27, to combat Omicron, 1st in the country
Argentine Vice-president Cristina Fernández de Kirchner found guilty of fraud, sentenced to six years in prison and banned from holding office
Donald Trump's real estate company Trump Organization convicted of tax fraud in New York
Indonesia passes a new criminal code outlawing sex outside marriage, even for foreigners
The 1,574th - and last - Boeing 747 jumbo jet rolls off of production line in Everett, Washington
South Fork Wind, the first large-scale offshore wind farm begins supplying electricity to the US, 35 miles off Montauk
Thomas Aquinas, thought to have had a mystical experience in Naples, refuses to continue his work "I cannot, because all that I have written seems like straw to me"
DonAlfonso Vof Aragon grants Barcelona the right to exclude Jews
PopeClement VIIescapes imprisonment in the Castel Sant'Angelo in Rome, fleeing to Orvieto disguised as a peddler
Johannes Kepleris the first to predict the transit of Venus, though no one observes it as it occurs after sunset in Europe
Holy Roman EmperorCharles VI's Pragmatic Sanction with the Diet of Hungary recognizes the King's daughters as successors
Bonnie Prince Charlie's Jacobite army retreats to Scotland
British troops underRobert Cliveoccupy Fulta in India
US PresidentJohn Quincy Adamssuggests establishment of a national observatory
Robert Schumann's 4th Symphony in D minor premieres
Hector Berlioz's dramatic oratorio "La Damnation de Faust" premieres, conducted by the composer at the Opéra-Comique, Paris
Harriet Tubmanescapes from slavery in Maryland for the second and final time
US PresidentAbraham Lincolnorders hanging of 39 Santee Sioux Indians
US Electoral College casts their votes in the disputed election betweenRutherford B. Hayesand Samuel J. Tilden; two sets of conflicting results are returned to Congress the next day
Theodore Rooseveltconfirms Monroe Doctrine (Roosevelt Corollary)
Canada's Liberal Party and its leaderMackenzie KingdefeatsArthur Meighen's Conservative Party and Thomas Crerar' Progressive Party in general election, goes on to form a minority government
1st US Presidential address broadcast on radio by PresidentCalvin Coolidge
Ban onJames Joyce's novel "Ulysses" in the US is lifted
Pietro Badoglioresigns as viceroy of Ethiopia
Queen Wilhelminaannounces Dutch Commonwealth
The Everglades National Park in Florida is dedicated by PresidentHarry S. Truman
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Mirabile illud
Simone de Beauvoirreceives the Prix Goncourt prize in French literature

Australian women's 4 × 100m freestyle relay team ofDawn Fraser, Faith Leech, Sandra Morgan & Lorraine Crapp swim world record 4:17.1 and beat powerful US team to win the gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics
Nelson Mandelaand 156 others are arrested for political activities in South Africa
Roger Sessions' 3rd Symphony premieres in Boston with Charles Munch conducting the Boston Symphony
27th Heisman Trophy Award:Ernie Davis, Syracuse RB; 1st African American to win)

PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonconfers Presidential Medal of Freedom on 31 recipients selected byJFK, including: contraltoMarian Anderson; diplomatRalph Bunche; cellist Pablo Casals; Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter; labor leader George Meaney; architect Mies van der Rohe; pianist Rudolf Serkin; writersE. B. Whiteand Thorton Wilder; and painter Andrew Wyeth; as well as posthumously to JFK himself, and PopeJohn XXIII
PresidentAntonio Segniof Italy resigns
Decca Records releases Rolling Stones album "Beggar's Banquet" in UK, the last duringBrian Jones' lifetime  (US release the next day)
300,000 attend a free concert featuringThe Rolling Stonesin Altamont, California; the event is marred by violence and four deaths
Gerald Fordsworn-in as first unelected Vice President, succeedsSpiro Agnewwho resigned over corruption allegations
George Harrisonreleases single "Ding Dong, Ding Dong"
Dutch war criminalPieter Mentenarrested in Zurich, Switzerland
Yasser Arafatmeets prominent American Jews in Stockholm, Sweden
Saddam Husseinannounces release of all foreign hostages
San Francisco 49ers wide receiverJerry Ricecatches NFL record 101st touchdown in a 27-3 win over the Miami Dolphins at Candlestick Park
San Francisco Giants sign record $43million USD pact with Pirates MLB outfielderBarry Bonds
"Dumb & Dumber", directed by the Farrelly brothers and starringJim Carreyand Jeff Daniels, premieres in Los Angeles
Michael Jacksoncollapses while rehearsing for an HBO special
Hugo Chávezis elected President of Venezuela
Great Court of the British Museum redesigned with the largest covered courtyard in Europe by Foster & Partners opened byQueen Elizabeth II
"Adaptation" directed by Spike Jonze, starringNicolas CageandMeryl Streeppremieres
American pin-up modelBettie Page(85) is hospitalized in critical condition after suffering a heart attack
Final Broadway performance of "A Steady Rain", starringHugh JackmanandDaniel Craig
Record price for a printed book when a first edition of "The Birds of America" byJohn James Audubonauctioned at Sotheby's, London, England, for £7,321,250
Pope Francisgives his ascent to a proposal to create a permanent post on the Pontifical Commission on cases of sin and sexual abuse of minors
Andrej Babišis sworn as Prime Minister of the Czech Republic
US PresidentDonald Trumpofficially recognizes Jerusalem as Israel's capital, announcing plans to move US embassy there

Tennessee Titans running backDerrick Henryexplodes for franchise-record 238 yards & 4 touchdowns in 30-9 win v Jacksonville Jaguars in Nashville; 99-yard TD run ties Dallas Cowboys HOF RB Tony Dorsett's longest run in NFL history
Former Myanmar leaderAung San Suu Kyisentenced to four years (later reduced to two) for inciting public unrest and breaching Covid-19 protocols
James Cameron's sci-fi film sequel "Avatar: The Way of Water" starringSam WorthingtonandZoe Saldañapremieres in London
Taylor Swiftis named Time's Person of the Year
Mongols led by Batu Khan occupy and destroy Kyiv after an eight-day siege; out of 50,000 people in the city, only 2,000 survive

Thomas Edisonenters the offices of Scientific American and turns the crank on his cylinder phonograph, astonishing those present with the recording, "Good morning. How do you do? How do you like the phonograph?"
Anglo-Irish Treatyis signed; Ireland receives dominion status; partition creates Northern Ireland
Hasan al-Askari, 11th Imam of Twelver Shia Islam, born in Medina, Abbasid Empire (d. 874)
Ferdinand IV, King of Castile and León (1295-1312), born in Seville, Spain (d. 1312)
Baldassare Castiglione, Italian diplomat and author, born in Casatico, Mantua, Italy (d. 1529)
Orazio Tiberio Vecchi, Italian composer, born in Modena, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (d. 1605)
Niccolò Zucchi, Italian astronomer, born in Parma, Italy in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (now Italy) (d. 1670)
William Cavendish, 1st Duke of Newcastle, English aristocrat and Royalist general in the English Civil War, born in Handsworth, England (d. 1676)
George Monck, English general (Civil War Royalist, Battle of Dunbar, Anglo-Dutch Wars), born in Great Potheridge, England (d. 1670)
Edmund Andros, English governor in North America, born in London (d. 1714)
Claude Fleury, French priest and historian, born in Paris (d. 1723)
Johann Christoph Bach, German harpsichordist and composer, born in Arnstadt, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1703)
Marie-Adélaïde of Savoy, mother of Louis XV of France, born in Royal Palace, Turin, Savoy (d. 1712)
Carlo Arrigoni, Italian lutenist, harpsichordist, and composer (Il Pentimento d’Accabo; Fernando), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1744)
Guillaume-Chrétien de Lamoignon de Malesherbes, French lawyer and statesman, born in Paris (d. 1794)
James Elphinston, Scottish philologist, born in Edinburgh (d. 1809)
Sophie von Laroche, German writer (History of Lady Sophia Sternheim), born in Kaufbeuren. Bavaria (d. 1807)
Warren Hastings, 1st governor-general of India (1773-84), born in Churchill, Oxfordshire (d. 1818)
Franz Nikolaus Novotny, Austrian composer, born in Eisenstadt, Austria (d. 1773)
Gabriel Duvall, Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court (1811-35), born in Marietta, Maryland (d. 1844)
Johann Baptist Henneberg, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1822)
Nicolas Isouard, French composer, born in Porto Salvo, Valletta, Malta (d. 1818)
Paul Friedrich Struck, composer, born in Stralsund, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1820)
Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac, French physicist and chemist, born in Saint-Léonard-de-Noblat, Kingdom of France (d. 1850)
Abraham Jacob van der Aa, Dutch lexicographer (Biographic Dictionary), born in Amsterdam (d. 1857)
William II, King of the Netherlands, Grand Duke of Luxembourg, and Duke of Limburg (1840-49), born in Noordeinde Palace, The Hague, Dutch Republic (d. 1849)
Adolf Reubke, German organ builder, born in Halberstadt, Germany (d. 1875)
Gilbert Duprez, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1896)
Johan Michiel Dautzenberg, Flemish author (Future), born in Heerlen, Netherlands (d. 1869)
Johann Christian Gebauer, Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1884)
Stephen Thomas, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Bethel, Vermont (d. 1903)
Robert Spear Hudson, English businessman, born in West Bromwich, Staffordshire (d. 1884)
Juan Prim, Spanish general and politician (Prime Minister, 1869-70), born in Reus, Catalonia (d. 1870)
Henry Eustace McCulloch, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Rutherford County, Tennessee (d. 1895)
John Eberhard Faber, German-American pencil manufacturer and built 1st large-scale pencil factory in US, born in Stein Bavaria, Germany (d. 1879)
Max Müller, German-born British orientalist, born in Dessau, German Federation (d. 1900)
Joshua Woodrow Sill, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Chillicothe, Ohio (d. 1862)
Rudolf Fittig, German chemist (synthesizing organic compounds), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1910)
Frédéric Bazille, French painter, born in Montpellier, Hérault, Languedoc-Roussillon, France (d. 1870)
Albert de Vriendt, Flemish historical painter and etcher, born in Ghent, belgium (d. 1900)
Henryk Jarecki, Polish composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1918)
August von Mackensen, German field marshal (WWI), born in Haus Leipnitz, Kingdom of Prussia, German Confederation (d. 1945)
Hara Prasad Shastri, Indian academic, Sanskrit scholar, archivist and historian of Bengali literature, born in Khulna, Bengal Presidency (d. 1931)
Charles Martin Hall, American chemist and investor, born in Thompson, Ohio (d. 1914)
William S. Hart, American silent film actor (Wild Bill Hickok; Tumbleweeds), born in Newburgh, New York (d. 1946)
Mikuláš Moyzes, Slovak composer (The Orphans), born in Zvolenská Slatina, Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia (d. 1944)
Evelyn Underhill, British Anglo-Catholic poet, born in Wolverhampton, England (d. 1941)
Fred Duesenberg, German-American automobile pioneer, born in Lippe, Germany (d. 1932)
(Jennie) Jane Morgan, British-American vaudeville, radio, and screen actress (Our Miss Brooks - "Mrs Margaret Davis"), born in Warmley, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1972)
Rose Schneiderman, American socialist, feminist, trade union leader and secretary of labor for New York State (1937-44), born in Sawin, Poland (d. 1972)
Albrecht Schaeffer, German writer (The General), born in Elbląg, Poland (d. 1950)
Joseph Lamb, American ragtime pianist and composer ("Bohemia Rag"), born in Montclair, New Jersey (d. 1960)[1][2]
Lynn Fontanne, British actress (Peter Pan; The Guardsman), born in Woodford, Essex, England (d. 1983)

Emiliana de Zubeldía, Spanish-Mexican concert pianist, composer - chiefly for guitar (Landscape Basko), and educator (Academy of Music at the University of Sonora, 1947-87), born in Salinas de Oro, Spain (d. 1987)
Will Hay [William Thomson Hay], British comedian, actor and amateur astronomer, born in County Durham, England (d. 1949)
George Street, English cricket wicket-keeper (1 Test, 1 dismissal; Surrey CCC, MCC), born in Charlwood, England (d. 1924)
Rudolf Schlichter, German artist and writer, born in Calw, Germany (d. 1955)
Yoshio Nishina, Japanese physicist (known as the founding father of modern physics research in Japan), born in Satoshō, Okayama (d. 1951)
Lina Carstens, German actress (Homeland, Broken Jug), born in Wiesbaden, Germany (d. 1978)
Osbert Sitwell, English poet, writer (Out of the Flame), born in London, England (d. 1969)

Henriëtte Bosmans, Dutch pianist and composer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1952)
Arnold Foster, English composer, born in Sheffield, England (d. 1963)
George Trafton, American NFL center (Chicago Bears), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1971)

Alfred Eisenstaedt, German-American photojournalist (V-J Day in Times Square), born in Dirschau, West Prussia, German Empire (d. 1995)
Gunnar Myrdal, Swedish sociologist and economist (Nobel 1974), born in Gagnef, Sweden (d. 1987)
Herman Shumlin, American Broadway producer, director and actor (Watch on the Rhine), born in Atwood, Colorado (d. 1979)

Nikolai Batalov, Russian actor (Tretya meshchanskaya, Mother), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1937)

Ève Curie, French author and daughter of Madame Curie, born in Paris (d. 2007)
Antoon Spinoy, Belgian politician, born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1967)

Pierre Graber, Swiss politician and member of the Swiss Federal Council (1970–1978), born in La Chaux-de-Fonds, Switzerland (d. 2003)
Alan McGilvray, Australian cricketer (20 games for NSW mid 1930's) and commentator (ABC), born in Paddington, Sydney, Australia (d. 1996)
Kenneth Watkins, British woodland conservationist and founder (The Woodland Trust), born in Bromley, Kent (d. 1996)
Alfred James Broomhall, British Protestant Christian medical missionary to China, born in Shandong, China (d. 1994)
Eleanor Holm, American 100m backstroke swimmer (Olympic gold 1932), born in New York City (d. 2004)

Mamo Clark, American actress (One Million BC; Robinson Crusoe of Clipper Island), and author, born in Honolulu, Territory of Hawaii (d. 1986)
Hugo Peretti, American songwriter and record producer ("Shout"; "Twistin' The Night Away"), born in New York City (d. 1986)
Irv Robbins, Canadian-American entrepreneur (co-founded the Baskin-Robbins ice cream parlor chain), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2008)
Kamal Jumblatt, Lebanese politician and founder of the Progressive Socialist Party, born in Moukhtara, Chouf, Mutasarrifate of Mount Lebanon, Ottoman Empire (d. 1977)
Harold Horace Hopkins, British physicist and inventor (Endoscope), born in Leicester, England (d. 1994)
Gideon Klein, Moravian pianist and composer, born in Přerov, Czechoslovakia (d. 1945)
Jimmy Bivins [James], Hall of Fame heavyweight boxer, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2012)

Willis Whitfield, American physicist and inventor of the clean room, born in Rosedale, Oklahoma (d. 2012)[1]

George Porter, British chemist (Nobel 1967), born in Stainforth, South Yorkshire, England (d. 2002)
Piero Piccioni, Italian jazz pianist, lawyer, film score composer (Swept Away), and conductor, born in Turin, Piedmont, Italy (d. 2004)
Guy Thys, Belgian longest-serving football coach (final UEFA European Championship 1980), born in Antwerp, Belgium[1]
John Brunt, English soldier and Victoria Cross holder, born in Priest Weston, Shropshire, England (d. 1944)

Alain Tanner, Swiss film director (Charles, Dead or Alive), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 2022)
Frank Springer, American comics artist (The Adventures of Phoebe Zeit-Geist), born in Jamaica, Queens, New York (d. 2009)

Mark Kopytman, Russian-Israeli composer (Voices of Memory), musicologist, and pedagogue (Jerusalem Academy of Music and Dance. 1974-94), born in Kamianets-Podilskyi, Soviet Union (now Ukraine) (d. 2011)
Nikolaus Harnoncourt, Austrian conductor (Early Music Movement), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2016)
Robert "King" Moody, American actor and comedian (Get Smart - "Shtarker"), born in New York City (d. 2001)
Wojciech Zabłocki, Polish fencer (4 x World C'ship gold team; Olympic silver team sabre 1956, 60; bronze 1964), born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 2020)
Zeki Müren, Turkish actor, singer, and composer (Game of Silence), born in Bursa, Turkey (d. 1996)
Henryk Górecki, Polish composer (Symphony No. 3 - Symphony of Sorrowful Songs), born in Czernica, Poland (d. 2010)
Jim Pothecary, South African cricket medium pace bowler (3 Tests, 9 wickets; Western Province), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 2016)
Nick Bockwinkel, American professional wrestler (WCW/AWA), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2015)
George Williams, American soul singer (The Tymes - "So Much in Love"; "Ms Grace"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2004)
Jean Lapointe, Quebecois-Canadian comedian, singer and senator, born in La Matanie, Quebec
David Ossman, American writer and comedian (The Firesign Theatre), born in Santa Monica, California
Kenneth Copeland, American televangelist (Kenneth Copeland Ministries), born in Lubbock, Texas
Alberto Spencer, Ecuadorian soccer forward (11 caps; 5 caps Uruguay; most goals in Copa Libertadores, 54 goals 1960-72), born in Ancón, Santa Elena, Ecuador (d. 2006)
Oxana Yablonskaya, Russian concert pianist, and music professor (Juilliard School, 1978-2009), born in Moscow, USSR
Patrick Bauchau, Belgian actor (Scarpine-A View To Kill), born in Brussels, Belgium
Steve Alaimo, American pop singer ("Mashed Potatoes"; "Every Day I Have To Cry"), TV host (Where the Action Is), and producer, born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 2024)
Tomáš Svoboda, Czech pianist and composer (Etude; Symphony of Nature), born in Paris, France (d. 2022)
Jay Leonhart, American session and touring jazz and pop double bassist and songwriter (The Bass Lesson), born in Baltimore, Maryland
Lawrence Bergman, Canadian politician, born in Montreal, Quebec
Helen Cornelius, American country music singer (Nashville on the Road), born in Monroe City, Missouri
